coredata.dc:title: Engineered graphene quantum dot nanocomposite triggers α-synuclein defibrillation: Therapeutics against Parkinson's disease
coredata.dc:creator: Kaliyaperumal, Poonkuzhali
Renganathan, Seenivasagan
Arumugam, Karthika
Aremu, Bukola Rhoda
coredata.dc:description: Emerging clinically required α-synuclein (α-syn) inhibitor which acts as a neuroprotective nanocomposite drug is in increased demand as a patient-safe central nervous system therapeutic. This inhibitor is intended to chemically engineer graphene quantum dot (GQD) with blue luminescence, and stands to be a potential cure for Parkinson's disease. It has been theorized that α-syn aggregation is a critical step in the development of Parkinson's. Hence narrow the target by α-syn inhibition, through chemically synthesize methyl N-allyl N-benzoylmethioninate (MABM) and functionally engineer the surface of GQD to target the brain delivery on C57BL/6 mice. Spectroscopic and simulation studies confirm defibrillation through the interaction between N-terminal amino acids and MABM-GQD nanoparticles, which makes nontoxic α-syn. Therefore, this drug's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier in vitro functionally prevents neuronal loss in neuroblastoma cells. Thus, in vivo cerebral blood flow analysis using magnetic resonance imaging illustrates, how this nanocomposite can possibly treat Parkinson's.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM and DLS histogram images of MABM-GQD NPs, 78.66 nm. B) UV–Visible absorption spectra of MABM-GQD NPs. C) XPS. D) IR. E) Raman. F) BDS spectrum for MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs.
A) MABM-GQD NPs releasing efficiency on medium. B) Role of MABM-GQD NPs on α-syn inhibition pathway.

A) α-Syn structure and its reaction mechanism plot with MABM-GQD NPs. B) α-Syn fibril disaggregation kinetics in the presence of MABM-GQD NPs at different time periods. Reaction was monitored using ThT assay. Sigmoidal dose dependent curve was fitted for this reaction (n = 3 biologically independent samples; error bars show mean ± s.d. two way ANOVA with Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, NS, not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 was considered as statistically significant). C) TEM study of α-syn PFFs incubated with MABM-GQD NPs for different time periods (0, 6, 12 and 24 h). D) α-Syn fibril end-to-end length and number of fibrils quantification per μm2. E) Raman spectrum for α-syn PFFs incubated with MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs for 24 h.
Raman 2D maps of A) α-syn PFFs incubated with GQD, B) α-syn PFFs, α-syn PFFs incubated with C) MABM, and D) MABM-GQD NPs for 24 h.

A) FTIR spectrum for α-syn PFFs incubated with MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs for 24 h. B) Dynamic simulation of interaction between α-syn fibrils and MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs. C) RMSD time dependent plot of average change in displacement of selection of atoms when α-syn fibril incubated with MABM-GQD NPs. D) Time line representation of amino acid residues interact with MABM-GQD NPs (residues make more than one specific contact with ligand, which is represented by a darker shade of orange, according to the scale on right of the plot).

A) Proposed scheme for vitro BBB co-culture model using HUVEC and SH-SY5Y cells. B) MTT assay in α-syn PFFs induced cytotoxicity of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs, evaluated on neuroblastoma cells (n = 3 biologically independent samples; error bars show mean ± s.d. two way ANOVA with Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, NS, not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 was considered as statistically significant). C) Fluorescent microscopy images shown recovery from α-syn PFFs induced cytotoxicity (first row — high green fluorescent, live cells and less green fluorescent, dead cells), DAPI staining for nucleus morphology (second row — high blue fluorescent, live cells and less blue fluorescent, dead cells) and ethidium homodimer staining (third row — high red fluorescent, dead cells and less red fluorescent, live cells). Scale bars are 50 μm. D) TEER assay. E) Permeability percentage for MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs crossing BBB over different time periods (n = 3 biologically independent samples; error bars show mean ± s.d. two way ANOVA with Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, NS, not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 was considered as statistically significant). F) Microscopical images of co-culture model with drugs.

A) α-syn PFFs (5 μg in 1 μl) stereotaxically injected at right dorsal striatum of C57BL/6 mice narrow down to bregma and dural surface; AP, + 0.3 mm; ML, + 2.3 mm; DV, − 3.5 mm, 50 μl of MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) were i.p. injected on weekly twice for 3 months as treatment with its individual control. B) Cylinder test for measuring the forepaw practice of mice. C) Rota rod test for assessing mice performance time and No of fall (n = 3 biologically independent samples; error bars show mean ± s.d. two way ANOVA with Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, NS, not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 was considered as statistically significant). D) α-Syn staining of STR and SN region of all mice groups. E) Quantification of α-syn at STR and SN region. F) TH+ immunoreactive neurons were measured in the striatum (n = 3 biologically independent samples; error bars show mean ± s.d. two way ANOVA with Bonferroni multiple comparisons test, NS, not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 was considered as statistically significant). G) MRI scan with CBF changes were seen using 3D ASL at coronal brain view of α-syn PFFs group possess weak blue signal; bad CBF flow whereas, MABM-GQD NPs shown strong red signal indicate good CBF flow.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging clinically required α-synuclein (α-syn) inhibitor which acts as a neuroprotective nanocomposite drug is in increased demand as a patient-safe central nervous system therapeutic. This inhibitor is intended to chemically engineer graphene quantum dot (GQD) with blue luminescence, and stands to be a potential cure for Parkinson's disease. It has been theorized that α-syn aggregation is a critical step in the development of Parkinson's. Hence narrow the target by α-syn inhibition, through chemically synthesize methyl N-allyl N-benzoylmethioninate (MABM) and functionally engineer the surface of GQD to target the brain delivery on C57BL/6 mice. Spectroscopic and simulation studies confirm defibrillation through the interaction between N-terminal amino acids and MABM-GQD nanoparticles, which makes nontoxic α-syn. Therefore, this drug's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier in vitro functionally prevents neuronal loss in neuroblastoma cells. Thus, in vivo cerebral blood flow analysis using magnetic resonance imaging illustrates, how this nanocomposite can possibly treat Parkinson's.

Parkinson's disease caused by α-synuclein protein fibrils storage in brain is a pathogenic hallmark for neurodegenerative disorders. We designed and chemically engineered the graphene quantum dot (GQD) using synthesized organic novel compound named methyl N-allyl N-benzoylmethioninate (MABM). MABM loaded GQD nanoparticles exhibit good in vitro α-synuclein defibrillation activity. The targeted delivery of nanocomposite effect in vitro Blood brain barrier model possesses excellent crossing ability of drug. In vivo Magnetic resonance imaging cerebral blood flow studies confirms that nanocomposite cure Parkinson's disease neurodegeneration through α-synuclein defibrillation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common degenerative disease, and is caused by the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra region and the incidence of Lewy bodies (LBs) as inclusions in the brain. 1 α-Syn aggregates are a major fibrillar component of LBs and represent critical pathway steps in the etiology of PD. 2 The α-syn mature fibrils rich in β-sheets are heterogeneous structures that form the early-stage oligomers and account for the most toxic assemblies in the brain. 3 The neuronal transmission of α-syn fibrils to adjacent nerves lead to overt cell loss or neuronal death. Based on current evidence, a compound that slows down or prevents α-syn fibrillation and aggregation could lead to a therapeutic tactic with the potential to cure PD. 3,4

Recently, sulfur allyl compounds were noted for their neuroprotective effect by several modes of action. 5,6 Notably, the clearance rate of allyl sulfur groups from cells is a determinant of the overall response. 7,8 The novel derivative contains a sulfur allyl group that has been synthesized from an amino acid, l-methionine, which possesses an active nitrogen functional group that is ready to bond with a nanocarrier for specialised blood-brain barrier (BBB) transmission. 9 On the other hand, as the tightest endothelial barrier in the body, the BBB is composed of an endothelial monolayer, pericytes and astrocytes at the abluminal side of the brain vessels. 10 Hydrophilic molecules that travel across the BBB force it to regulate their passage; in addition, nanoparticles have a significant effect owing to the uncomplicated targeted delivery of drug to brain. 11

Therefore, choosing a nanocarrier to target specific drug delivery is vital for treating neurological diseases. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is an evolving material unique owing to its functional groups, as well as electrical and optical properties. 12 It has great bioavailability owing to its size (1–15 nm), simple preparation method, low cytotoxicity, higher photostability, and hydrophilic nature. 13 In addition, GQD was reported to have individual α-syn inhibition activity against α-synopathy in PD. 14

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), an image-guided therapeutic instrument used to detect and diagnose diseases like cancer, neurological disorders, etc., should be applied in biomedical research. 15 Innovative advances in biometabolic engineering, besides chemically engineering the blue luminescent GQD using a synthesized drug, have produced efficient nanocomposites for treatment against neurodegeneration in PD. This nanoparticulate can act as a dual-mode application as: α-syn inhibition power and BBB drug delivery. Likewise, the scientific community's ever-growing ambition in theranostic techniques requires they look for more complex all-in-one nanomaterial they can use for challenging medical tasks. Thus synthesized methionine derivative called methyl N-allyl N-benzoylmethioninate (MABM) was modified in the GQD nanoparticles to produce engineered nanocomposite smaller than 100 nm. Furthermore, the applications of novel engineered methyl N-allyl N-benzoylmethioninate-graphene quantum dot nanoparticles (MABM-GQD NPs) on α-syn defibrillation, guided by MRI have been studied extensively in vitro and in vivo.

A combination of 100 mmol (1.4921 g) l-methionine, and 26 mmol (~2 ml) thionyl chloride were added as drops to 30 ml cold methanol (−5 °C), which was stirred continuous under 4 °C and kept under reflux for 3 h. The solvent was under reduced pressure. The white solid crystals of methionine dimethyl ester were obtained from the oil residue by recrystallization from (methanol:diethylether) in a yield of 84 %.To the ester 1.0 g, one equivalent of allylbromide and pyridine was added under dry dimethylformamide (DMF). The mixture was filtered after 3 h of stirring at room temperature and filtered. The filtrate was concentrated to a small volume in vacuo (40 °C) and filtered again. The resulting solid was washed repeatedly with ethanol, dried in vacuo, and recrystallized with water/ethanol (5:6) to yield white crystal needles of methyl allylmethioninate at 68 %. The resultant product was benzoylated using two equivalents of benzoyl chloride and pyridine under dry DMF at room temperature for 2 h. The product was then washed with cold water, and dried under vacuum. It appears as white crystals and was called MABM.

MABM was loaded on GQD (Blue luminescent, Sigma Aldrich, USA) by adding five times increased concentration of MABM 1.25 mg/ml with 250 μg/ml GQD at pH 9.0 and reflux at 60 °C, for 3 h. Continuing reflux, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 17,530 ×g for 20 min. After washing several times with DI water, the resulting pellet was dried under vacuum and the MABM-GQD NPs were collected by recrystallization of unattached MABM by evaporation of the supernatant.

The equation used to calculate the drug loading efficiency of MABM in GQD is as follows: DL = [(Winitial MABM − Wfree MABM) / (WGQD) (mg/g) 2 , where Winitial MABM-1250 μg, Wfree-237 μg, WGQD-250 μg where the drug loading efficiency of MABM in GQD was 40,520 mg/g for 1.25 mg of MABM, respectively. The in vitro drug release profiles of MABM-GQD NPs were tested against different pH levels (5, 7.5, and 9) and lengths of time (5, 10, 15, 20 and 24 h) at 37 °C. Based on the weight of the released MABM, the leftover drug on the surface of GQD was calculated.

All NMR spectra were recorded with Bruker II-400 (Bruker BioSpin GmbH, Rheinstetten, Germany) instruments, using tetramethylsilane as an internal standard and CDCl3, D2O as the solvent. MABM's melting point (Mp) was determined in an open capillary with electrothermal MEL-TEMP 3.0.

In addition to the above experiments, MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; TECNAI 10, Philips, Dutch), dynamic light scattering particle size analyser (Zeta Sizer Nano S, Malvern, UK), Ultra Violet-Visible spectrometer (UV-VIS; Shimadzu 3600, CA, US), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), PHI-5000 VersaProbe. Furthermore, high resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) data were obtained on an Accurate-Mass Q-TOF LC/MS (Agilent, Santa Clara, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: α-Syn fibrillation studies and thioflavin T assay
ce:section.ce:para: α-Syn monomers and preformed fibrils (PFFs) were purchased from Enzo Life Sciences (ALX-201-265) and Stress Marq Biosciences Inc. (SPR-322). MABM (100 mM) was dissolved in 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) for the stock preparation. To assess α-syn fibrillation, α-syn monomers (50 μM) were mixed with a working concentration of MABM (5, 10, 15, 30 and 60 μM), GQD (10, 25, 50, 100 and 125 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (1, 2.5, 5, 10 and 20 μM) and were dissolved in PBS buffer (pH 7.4); the control α-syn solution contained 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). Aggregation was performed in a temperature-controlled shaking incubator (ThermoMixer, Hamburg, Germany) at 37 °C, shaking at 150 rpm. For the defibrillation assay, α-syn PFFs were mixed with the same abovementioned concentration of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs. Aliquots (100 μl) were removed from the incubated solution and added to 500 μl of 10 μM ThT solution in PBS (pH 7.4) as a function of time to monitor the α-syn fibrillation kinetics. Using a FluoroMax-3 Spectrofluorimeter (Jobin Yvon Horiba Scientific, Edison, New Jersey, USA), ThT fluorescence was measured at 482 nm through excitation at 450 nm. The slits measuring 2.5 nm remained the same during both excitation and emission. The length and quantity of α-syn fibrils were determined over a different time period. A heat map was also generated to validate the end-to-end fibril length. All aggregations and disaggregation measurements were performed in triplicate. The drug dosages for fibrillation and defibrillation were obtained by lag times estimated from the sigmoidal dose-dependent curve fitting model.
TEM analysis.

The morphology of the α-syn PFF (50 μM) incubated with MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs (60, 150, 10 μM, 24 h) were observed using TEM (FEI Tecnai G220 S-TWIN, Hillsboro, OR, USA). α-Syn defibrillation was studied at different time periods through 3–5 μl of the sample placed on copper grids and negatively stained with 2 % phosphotungstic acid for 30 s. After the sample was stained, the grid was dehydrated to a temperature under 37 °C for better result.

After incubating the α-syn PFF (50 μM) with MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) for 24 h in PBS buffer (20 mM; pH 7.4). We measured the reaction contents using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Perkin Elmer, USA) at wavelengths ranging from 450 cm−1 to 5000 cm−1.

α-Syn fibrils (50 μM) in the presence and absence of MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs (60, 150, 10 μM, 24 h) were deposited onto the surface of 1 cm2 silicon dioxide substrate as 25 μl droplets. Raman mapping images were captured using a confocal unit Renishaw inVia Raman Microscope (Renishaw plc, Gloucestershire, UK) by argon-ion laser (532 nm) with 3.06 mW power (1 s exposure time on 20× objective). After removing the buffer background that was recorded under similar circumstances, all Raman spectra were adjusted using a baseline polynomial fit (WiRE 5.2 software, Eurotech, S.P.A, Italy). Raman imaging was performed using a 514 nm laser, with 1.60 mW power and 3 to 5 s exposure time while acquiring with a 60× water immersion objective for 30 scan. Raman 2D maps were created based on the second order of the D band mode by obeying the momentum conservation rule, which initiates the double resonance process.

Impedance spectroscopy (Concept 80, NOVOCONTROL Technologies GmbH & Co. Montabaur, Germany) studies were performed using 20 mM PBS buffer, pH 7.4, 24 h in electrochemical liquid sample cell. The samples placed were MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) with and without α-syn PFFs (50 μM). Impedance was recorded between frequency ranges up to 1 × 10−5 Hz. At isothermal temperature (37 °C), the data were collected and maintained through a Quatro Cryosystem (for low-temperature measurement), at a range between −180 °C and 400 °C. Nyquist plot was simulated using Z-view software (Scribner Associates, Inc., Southern Pines, NC, USA).

The molecular simulation was performed using Schrodinger software for 100 ns. Docking was run through the Autodock Vina program and the α-syn fibril interaction were examined at a molecular level with MABM, GQD, and MABM + GQD NPs. The NMR structure of α-syn (PDB ID: 2N0A) was obtained from CHARMM25 force field, and the structure of GQD was designed with CGenFF26 using protocols from https://cgenff.paramchem.org.

SH-SY5Y (NCCS, Pune) was cultured in DMEM/F12 with 10 % fetal bovine serum and penicillin (100 U/ml)-streptomycin (100 μg/ml) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere enriched with 5 % CO2. SH-SY5Y cell endurance was evaluated using MTT assay. Cells (103 cells/well) were seeded into 96-well plates. After 24 h, old media was replaced with PBS buffer (control), α-syn PFFs (50 μM), MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) as treated cells. Following a 48 h incubation period, the cells were treated for 3 h at 37 °C with MTT (1 mg/ml; Sigma-Aldrich), after which formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μl DMSO. The absorbance was measured at 490 nm using Microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). The percentage of viable cells was calculated using the following formula: (%) = [100 × (A abs) / (B abs)], where A and B represent the number of cells treated with or without MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs, respectively. The MTT assay was performed three times.

Following the MTT assay procedure, instead of adding MTT, the cells were washed with PBS, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, permeabilized with 0.1 % Triton X-100 and then stained with 2 μg/ml of Calcein-AM, DAPI and Ethidium homodimer-1 for 10 min at 37 °C in a dark environment. The fluorescent images of treated samples were obtained using a fluorescent microscope (Nikon Instruments Inc., New York, USA).

The HUVEC (ATCC, USA) and SH-SY5Y cells were plated onto 75 cm2 T-flasks separately, with a complete DMEM culture medium until they reached confluence. Transwell chamber embeds with a 0.4-μm opening (Corning, New York, USA) were placed into volumetric flasks. SH-SY5Y (1 ml; 1 × 106/ml) were added to the underside of the Transwell chamber embeds and incubated at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 for 24 h. After this, chamber compartments were positioned into a six-well plate. When the SH-SY5Y cells reached 60 % confluence under an inverted microscope, HUVEC cells (1 × 106/ml) were seeded on top of the transwell for continuous growth until they reached a high-density co-culture.

Before adding α-syn PFFs with MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs (60, 150, and 10 μM, respectively), the transepithelial electric resistance (TEER) value was determined. After 2, 4, 6, 12, and 24 h of drug incubation, the blood and brain sides of the medium were measured for TEER assay using ERS-2 Volt-Ohm Meter (Millipore, Boston, MA, USA). The permeability (%) calculations were analysed for their nanocomposite content using an UV spectrophotometer. α-syn fibrils were then quantified using ThT assay. The morphology of cells was recorded using an inverted biological microscope (MICAM software, SciMedia, Tokyo, Japan).

All animal studies were conducted in accordance with procedures approved by the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (IAEC) after receiving approval from the Committee for Control and Supervision of Experiments (CPCSEA) at the Department of Pharmacology, K. M. College of Pharmacy, Madurai, India (Registration No. 661/PO/Re/S/02/CPCSEA). C57BL/6 mice (8 to 10 week old males) were obtained from CAPE BIO Research Centre, Marthandam. The breeder's registration number was 245/PO/RCB/B1/S/18/CPCSEA. Animal gender was randomly selected for this experiment. It was ensured that all for the laboratory animals were treated according to the high ethical and scientific standards of CPCSEA guidelines. They were housed properly, and the method of drug injection was determined by measuring the drug's concentration (7 and 14 days). For the acute toxicity study, animals were divided into four groups where each group received a dosage (10, 100, and 1000 mg/kg of MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs), except the control group. They were observed for 14 days for signs of toxicity. The mice were then divided into four groups with three animal in each group: group I — was dosed α-syn PFFs, group II — was dosed α-syn PFFs + MABM, group III — was dosed α-syn PFFs + GQD, and group IV — was dosed α-syn PFFs + MABM-GQD NPs.

The mice were anesthetized with isoflurane and were then placed in a stereotaxic fixed frame. α-Syn PFFs (5 μg in 1 μl) were injected into the right dorsal striatum of the animals, utilizing the following stereotaxic coordinates relative to bregma and the dural surface: anterior-posterior (AP), + 0.3 mm; medial-lateral (ML), + 2.3 mm and dorsal-ventral (DV), − 3.0 mm. For 3 months, 50 μl of MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) were i.p. injected three times weekly. After three months, mice were euthanized and perfused, and 4 % PFA was used for fixation. The brains, livers, kidneys, and spleens were removed and fixed in 4 % PFA for 24 h and then underwent cryopreservation in 30 % sucrose.

Each mouse was sequentially administered with and without last dose of MABM-GQD NPs as contrast agent and immediately anesthetized using 10 % chloral hydrate (0.3 ml/100 g) then placed supine in the stage with the top of its head centred in a circular MRI coil. The stage was then placed within the MRI system (Magnex/Varian self-shielded 30 cm bore 7 Tesla magnets with a Research Resonance Instruments, Magnex Scientific Ltd., Abington, UK, Aarthi Scan Centre, Thirunelveli and the GE HealthCare “Micro”-Signa software environment).

The MRI scan provides greyscale images of the mice brains. We used 3D arterial spin labelling analysis (ASL) to obtain 15 slices in ascending order (slice thickness = 4 mm), field of view = 120 × 120 mm, matrix = 512 (points) × 12 (arms); number of excitations = 5, bandwidth = 62.5 kHz, scan duration was 9 min 14 s, labelling duration = 1650 ms, post labelling delay = 1025 ms, repetition time = 4132 ms, and echo time = 11 ms. Cerebral blood flow (CBF) was captured by time-of-flight angiography with a 3D Fast SPGR for which the scan parameters were as follows: echo time = 3.9 ms, repetition time = 20 ms, field of view = 80 × 60 mm, matrix, number of excitations = 320 × 224, one bandwidth = 31.2 kHz, and scan duration was 231 s. After scanning, the mice were permitted to recuperate in their cages. MRI data were viewed and analysed using Syngo fastview (Siemens AG Medical Solutions, Department SW, Erlangen) exclusively for DICOM images.

Each experiment was performed at least three times. The data values were stated as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis included two-way analysis of variance, which was performed using GraphPad Prism 9.0 (GraphPad Software, Inc., CA, US). Two way ANOVA and Bonferroni multiple-comparisons test was performed for assessing P values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical synthesis and characterization of MABM
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme S1 explains the synthesis of methionine derivative using ally bromide yields intermediate methyl allylmethioninate next to benzoylation yields MABM. The benzoylation process is essential to tailoring its bond formation with GQD. The choice of nanocarrier, blue luminescent GQD, bonded with MABM shows a remarkable α-syn inhibition ability, as shown in the MRI scans. The structure of MABM was synthesized with good yield and elucidated by 1H NMR, 13C, DEPT, FT IR, and HRMS analysis (Figs. S1–S4). MABM: Yield: 68 %, HRMS (ESI) calculated for C16H22NO3S [M + H] + 308.1320, found 308.1313.

Fig. S5 represents the mechanism of MABM loaded on GQD. Fig. S6A shows that the MABM loaded on GQD ratio ability was high at pH 9.0 and the drug retained on carrier has been assessed over diverse time and pH. The loading capacity was increased at basic pH and higher drug concentration (95 % of MABM loaded GQD at 24 h; Fig. S6B).

TEM and DLS analysis exhibit the MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. 1A and Fig. S7A) average particle size as being around 78.66 nm whereas for the control MABM, the average particle size was 41.83 nm and GQD 15.50 nm (Fig. S7B). The UV spectrum of MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs are displayed in Fig. 1b. For GQD, the typical absorption peak at 227 nm was consigned to the π-π* of aromatic CC bonds, and another n-π* broad absorption band at 350 nm was observed for heteroatoms (CO) shoulder. The MABM loading on GQD was confirmed by mass spectrum value of MABM before 181.06 and after 458.21 (Fig. S7C). The mass spectrum provides evidence of GQD bonding with MABM, and the loading capacity of that bond can be calculated as 40,800 mg/g by using the theoretical equation. Table S1 shows the comparison of fluorescence parameters among native GQD and engineered GQD using MABM through its photoluminescence. The fluorescence quantum yield was as high as 46.3 % (excited at 425 nm).

The chemical composition and chemical state of the elements of MABM-GQD NPs were further ascertained by XPS. The atomic ratio of engineered GQD consists of 67.67 % C, 2377 % N, 19.96 % O, and 3.21 % S (Fig. 1C). The corresponding peaks of C1s, N1s, O1S, and S1s can be observed at 287.6, 399.5, 532.6, and 283.2 eV, respectively. C1s spectrum displayed the existence of CC, CN, CO, or CO bonds, CS peaks at 287.6, 286.4, 289.8, and 287.9 eV (Fig. S7A). Fig. S7B exhibits the N1s spectrum with presence of nitrogen peak at 399.4 eV. Fig. 1d shows the FTIR study of the engineered GQD as it consists of OH, NH, aromatic CH, SH, CO, CN, CO and CC vibration band at 3435, 3392, 3068, 2562, 1760, 1688, 1294, and 934. The frequency of OH and NH bonds were shifted to higher values in the engineered GQD when compared to GQD, whereas the other bonds were shifted to lower values in the engineered GQD.

Two characteristic D and G peaks of MABM-GOD NPs were noted at wavenumbers ~1345 and ~1585 cm−1 with an intensity ratio of 0.75 (Fig. 1E). While MABM spectra compared with GQD (~1345 and ~1585 cm−1 with an intensity ratio of 0.63), the intensity ratio for the synthesized nanocomposite was greater because of introduction of MABM through the OH group. This confirms the slight disorder of sp2 clusters at the edges of GQD during the functionalization process. 16
Fig. 1F shows that the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of MABM-GQD NPS was ~52 Ω, which is less compared with MABM at, ~70 Ω, and GQD at, ~104 Ω and elucidates the decrease in engineered GQD Rct owing to quantum confinement with charge carriers of the graphene base.

For chemically engineered GQD to be absorbed it must first be dissolved in the aqueous environment of the gastrointestinal tract. Thus, a study of the dissolution of the drug dosage is a necessary criterion for determining the drug bioavailability of its in vivo performance. Disintegration times for different formulation concentrations of MABM and GQD were shown to be considerably increased at high MABM concentrations (200 μg/dl-18 min). The average mean of drug dissolution profiles observed for MABM-GQD NPs was high at 93 % for low concentrations of MABM, whereas for high concentration it should be decreased over D90 min (% drug release at 90 min) with a reversed function for T50% (min). The 200, 50 μg/dl concentration of MABM and GQD NPs showed sustained release (76 %) at 39 min. Table S2 demonstrates the constant drug release was optimal at pH 7.4 for 24 h. The sustained mean % release of MABM-GQD NPs at 2 h (pH 7.4) was 78 % (Table S2). The data reveals that dissolution testing of engineered GQD with controlled release in shorter time periods should commence, as it has promising drug characteristics.

The drug releasing model obeyed the principle of sustained release system (Fig. 2B). The mathematical kinetic model for drug dissolution follows first order kinetics for an extended period of time. MABM was released slowly, depending on the drug concentration. When the log percentage of the residual MABM-GQD NPs to be delivered versus time was plotted as per the first-order equation, straight lines were acquired (r2 = 0.66) to confirm that the drug-releasing model obeys first-order kinetics. The metabolic mechanism of MABM-GQD NPs on α-syn inhibition pathway is depicted in Fig. 2B.

The derivative prepared from l-methionine named MABM engineered GQD aimed to inhibit α-syn as a treatment for PD. In vitro and in vivo characteristics of MABM-GQD NPs were tested against α-syn activity in comparison with GQD and MABM alone at the same doses. The aggregation ability of α-syn was explored by incubating α-syn (50 mM) in the presence and absence of MABM (60 μM, 3 %), GQD (150 μM, 82 %), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM, 1 % at 24 h) at different concentrations to see how reduced fibril formation (Fig. S9A–C) occurred over varying time periods. Fig. 3A shows the defibrillation scheme for MABM-GQD NPs and its activity plot. α-Syn inhibition was achieved using nanocomposite in a dose-dependent manner and thioflavin T signal was reduced to 78 % with MABM-GQD NPs at 10 μM for 3 h, whereas with MABM it was reduced to, 48 % and with GQD it was reduced to, 15 % (Fig. 3B and Fig. S9D, E). From defibrillation sigmoidal curve, the MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) dosage concentrations were fixed for further study. SDS PAGE analysis noted the disappearance of α-syn fibrils (Fig. S10A). Fig. 3C shows TEM images that prove that the long needle-like fibrils were trimmed to short fragments that will subsequently disappear over time (Fig. S10B). The length of α-syn fibrils were shortened using MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs from, 5 μM to 67 nm, to 166 nm, and to 2 nm at 24 h, respectively (Fig. 3D). The number of α-syn fragments increased during incubation with MABM-GQD NPs, compared with MABM and GQD, in which the, whereas, average number of disassociated α-syn fibrils increased. A heat map of α-syn fibril fluorescence was acquired after ThT assay, indicating that the deleterious effects of α-syn PFFs were reduced by MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. S11A). AFM results confirm using distinct line graphs plotted, that the thickness of α-syn fibrils were reduced below 1 nm when incubated with MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. S11B, C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic analysis of engineered GQD nanocomposite with α-syn
ce:section.ce:para: On Raman spectral analysis, the bandwidth and shape of amide-I region at 1667 cm−1 (CO stretch of peptide backbone) was slightly shifted when α-syn was incubated with MABM and GQD, whereas, in the presence of MABM-GQD NPs, average deformation took place compared with the individual spectrum. The delicate amide-III peak conserved the α-syn fibril structure. However, its influence was modified by coupled CN stretch and NH bend (shifts from 1245 to 1228 cm−1), slight involvement of CCN deformations cause disaggregation in the order of MABM-GQD NPs < MABM < GQD respectively (Fig. 3E). The band disappearance at 1401 and 1450 cm−1 (CO2 symmetric stretch and CH2CH3 deformation) region in the presence of MABM-GQD NPs corroborates the fact that fibrils may become soluble. In addition to the amide-I and III regions, the deformation of the band at 1556 cm−1 elucidates that MABM-GQD NPs degraded the α-syn fibril structure. The peak at 1350 and 1500 cm−1 shows both the D and G band. Amide-III region deformation has been represented by the 2D map in Fig. 4A–D. A fairly blue shift has occurred when α-syn incubated with MABM-GQD NPs yields disassociated fibrils. The 2D map intensity peak range of 1455 to 1470 cm−1 is depicted in Fig. S12A–D.

The conformational properties of α-syn fibril defibrillation were analysed through FTIR spectrum in the presence of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs at 400 to 4000 cm−1 above the same wavenumber. The relative match observed between α-syn fibril and MABM-GQD NPs indicates that the NH stretch was shifted in the 1650 cm−1 region. In turn, the peak at 1629 cm−1 displays irregular α-syn fibril structure, similar to the appearance of its soluble form (Fig. 5A). Drastic changes were witnessed by creating unfolded soluble α-syn while incubating with MABM-GQD NPs. Circular dichroism explains the disaggregation of α-syn fibril, and the secondary structure displays a negative band ~210 nm and a positive band at ~194 nm, which are characteristic of β-sheet rich conformity present in the fibrillar arrangement (Fig. S13A). The soluble form of α-syn monomer band was observed at 194 nm when incubation with MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. S13B).

Fig. S13C describes the Nyquist plot spectrum for the α-syn control, which forms a semicircle over real impedance (Z′) along with its tested inhibitors. However, MABM-GQD NPs treated α-syn shows the decrease in the radius of semicircle indeed MABM and GQD shows less fall in forming semicircle. The linear regression fit for the Nyquist plot explains the intercept/slope values: α-syn (147.61 ± 0.49), MABM (64.769 ± 13.42), GQD (98.04 ± 5.18), and MABM-GQD NPs (47.49 ± 16.16) have been in reduced order through the defibrillation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamic approach of MABM-GQD NPs on α-syn
ce:section.ce:para: α-Syn fibril dissociation using MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs was analysed using Autodock Vina. The N-terminal cross-β part of the α-syn region commonly create bonds with MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs. All compounds primarily interact with the conserved region of amino acids. In MABM, four hydrogen bonds strongly interact with α-syn fibril of Gly86, Lys96, Gln99; hydrophobic interaction with (alkyl, pi-aikyl) and vander Waals interaction with Lys96, Phe94 and Ile88, Gly86, respectively. In GQD, a carbon‑hydrogen bond was formed through α-syn fibril of Gly86, hydrophobic interaction (pi-sigma, amide-pi stacked, alkyl), and vander Waals interaction with Gly86, Ala85, Lys96, and Ala85, Gly86, Phe94, Lys96, Asn103, Glu105, respectively. In MABM-GQD NPs, Fig. 5B shows how the hydrogen bond formed with the α-syn fibril of Asn103, Gly86; electrostatic, hydrophobic interaction with (alkyl, pi-orbitals) and Vander Waals interaction with Lys96, Ala85, and Ala85, Gly86, Phe94, Lys96, Asn103, Glu105, Glu106. Fig. 5C and Fig. S13D and E depict how the Root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) plot was calculated in the presence of MABM, GQD and MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. 5D, Fig. S13F, G) and predicts the molecular dynamics of α-syn fibril with drugs for 100 ns. From the result, it was concludes that MABM-GQD NPs were effectively destroyed within 20 ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of MABM-GQD NPs on neuroblastoma cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6A explains how neuroblastoma cells can be used to assess the drug targeting property. MTT reduction assay (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) was performed by human neuroblastoma cell line (SHSY5Y) induction using α-syn oligomers. Fig. 6B shows how the MTT reduction was decreased to 32 % upon α-syn PFFs (3 μM) treatment without drugs. However, in addition of MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) it enhances the reduction process corresponds to 81 %, 63 % and 92 %, respectively. Cytotoxicity of MABM-GQD NPs and its individual drug shows <15 % compared with preformed α-syn fibrils alone. These results support the hypothesis that MABM-GQD NPs have the ability to boost the α-syn fibrils defibrillation, which is what comprises the conversion mechanism that changes it from toxic to non-toxic soluble form. Upon incidence, LDH release was increased in α-syn PFFs treatment (83 %), whereas in the presence of MABM-GQD NPs-treated α-syn PFFs only displays 5 % (Fig. S14A). The data imply that nanocomposites and their individual drugs are non-toxic under the conditions studied. Cell viability was assessed using calcein AM/dihydroethidium staining upon pre-treatment of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD with α-syn PFFs (Fig. 6C, Fig. S14B). The number of viable cells with green fluorescence was higher in those treated with MABM-GQD NPs compared with controls. DAPI dye stained the adenine-thymine region in the nucleus DNA, which exhibits great blue fluorescence once cells incubate with MABM-GQD NPs. Consequently, dead cells were observed when incubated with dihydroethidium (1 μM) for 15 min, which results in MABM-GQD NPs as reduced red fluorescent and Western blot analysis confirmed the above results (Fig. 6C, Fig. S14C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BBB model crossing effect of MABM-GQD NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Tailored nanocomposite sizing optimizes properties like hydrodynamic size, surface charges, and conductive transport to the area of intended application (Fig. 6A). Water-soluble GQD act as a carrier to transport MABM across the BBB. The concentration of MABM-GQD NPs was greater in the brain side compared with the blood side. Subsequently, TEER intensity was quantified to validate the functional appearance of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs (Fig. 6D). The permeability of each drug in vitro was measured using UV spectrophotometer intensity on the brain side. Intriguingly, the intensity signal gradually increased with time, and showed 100 % permeability after 24 h (Fig. 6E). Microscopy images of the BBB model along with the drugs is shown in Fig. 6F. The endocytosed amount of MABM-GQD NPs was higher in the brain side, though the variance of drug concentration mean was assessed using Welch's test (Fig. S15A, B). Reduced amounts of α-syn fibrils was quantified using ThT assay to check the MABM-GQD NPs competence once again (Fig. S15C).

Black C57BL/6 mice stereotaxically injected with α-syn PFFs was the chosen PD model. Injection routes for drug administration were assessed before α-syn PFFs toxicity via intraperitoneal and intravenously for the dosage of MABM (60 μM), GQD (150 μM), and MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) (Fig. S15D). The multiple intraperitoneal injections of MABM-GQD NPs (10 μM) elicited highest quantity of its own in the mouse brains (Fig. S15E). Acute toxicity studies were used to determine the concentration of synthesized drug that could be used to optimise the dosage that would produce a detrimental effect on animals. Table S3 describes the safe dose (LD50) of MABM-GQD NPs was up to 1000 mg/kg body weight because no adverse effects were seen on the parameters assessment such as, body weight, food, water intake, mortality, and symptoms in the mice groups. To address the behavioural effect on C57BL/6 mice, cylinder and rotarod tests were demonstrated before performing the MRI scan. Weekly thrice administration of MABM-GQD NPs group significantly improved the locomotor and rearing activity; 95 % of PD motor symptoms were retrieved (Fig. 7A–C).

Fig. 7D points out increased α-syn expression in the PFFs-injected group, whereas the MABM-GQD NPs-administered group shows the lowest visible expression, which is the same as their individual control groups. We examined the α-syn PFF-induced TH neuron degeneration to elucidate its toxicity in administration of MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs towards the striatum and SNc region (Fig. 7E). The number of TH-positive striatal fibres was loss in absence of drugs; nevertheless, in the presence of MABM-GQD NPs tested along with the control, it prevents the decreasing level of TH+ neurons (Fig. 7Ff, S16A). Fig. S16B shows that the number of brain immune cells, such as GFAP-positive astrocytes, was considerably increased in the neocortex of α-syn PFFs-induced mice and decreased with the treatment of MABM-GQD NPs.

The survival rate of all mouse groups was assessed and interpreted using the Beckman curve, which proves that all mice (other than the α-syn PFFs-induced mice group) had a straight line until the period of drug administration (Fig. S16C). Both MABM and MABM-GQD NPs provided a survival advantage over the control animals, but the greatest survival was seen with a combined therapy strategy. With the major assessment of NP effects in the brain complete, side effects were determined by H&E staining of the mice liver, kidney, and spleen (Fig. S17A–F). There was no major structural alterations caused by the NPs compared with the controls.

MRI has been used to map the neurobiological substrate of motor and cognitive signs and symptoms in PD via T2 MRI. α-Syn PFF mice confirmed neurological degeneration through lack of CBF signal in a lateral-to-medial gradient instance of PD. CBF was seen in the cortex, striatum, and hippocampus regions of the brain in mice with PD. After three months of injecting MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs, the MRI was noticed (Fig. 7G). The red signal was detected in normal PBS, MABM, GQD, and MABM-GQD NPs mice groups, whereas in the α-syn PFFs-injected group, a weak blue signal was detected. Treatment groups with MABM exhibited green and yellow signal (middle good), GQD groups indicated blue signal (bad), and MABM-GQD NPs groups showed a strong red signal (good). There was a total increase in CBF signal for MABM-GQD NPs compared with its control. The resolution influence of MABM-GQD NPs treatment on mice illustrates spectacle signal intensity, a perfect vision of the neurodegeneration stage.

Parkinson's disease is perhaps the most widely recognized neurodegenerative disorder, affecting about 6.1 million patients worldwide. α-Syn aggregation, the neuropathological sign of PD, has been generally connected to neurotoxic pathways, eventually leading to neurodegeneration. 17,18 Until now, no profoundly powerful treatment has been accessible, which explains the expanding interest in the pursuit of a new restorative drug. We designed new hit-compounds for knock down of α-syn in vitro and in vivo environments. A sulfur-containing amino acid derived from garlic has been reported to be clinically important in diseases like diabetes, lipidemia, and cancer. 8 Substantial evidence supports the ability of garlic and its allyl sulfur compounds to act as protectors of immunocompetence and mental function. 19,20 In addition the high neuroprotective effects of organosulfur compounds with a thioallyl structure (-S-CH2CHCH2) have already been reported. 21 Thus, we chose to use the L-methionine derivative methyl N-allyl-N-benzoylmethioninate (MABM) to synthesize an allyl group, electronegative atom S, O, and nucleophilic aromatic substitution through benzoyl chloride bears elevated loading efficiency with GOD. 22 Nevertheless it possesses oxygen-containing functional groups aimed to make the bonding between surfaces of graphene base plane and synthesized derivative more efficient.

The interaction between oxygen-containing functional groups of GQD with excluding OH group of MABM (all other functional groups were sealed with trimethyl amide) make these nanoparticles more precise for use in targeted drug design. According to previous reports, GQD possesses the highest curcumin-loading capacity compared with other graphene derivatives. 23 In contrast doxorubicin loaded GQD 24 has great loading efficiency with targeted cancer therapeutic. The UV spectrum of engineered GQD has a shifted peak at 425 nm, which shows increased absorption intensities due to the increasing content of aromatic rings together with the allyl group presence from the MABM structure. 25 The heteroatoms in MABM with nitrogen and sulfur molecules is too effective at enhancing the quantum yield through chemical edge modification of graphene sheets. 26 Electrochemical studies reveal a semicircle in the high frequency region, which corresponds to limited electron transference and its dependency on the diffusion process. 27 However, the allyl and tertiary amide structures of MABM facilitates the fastest electron transport and shortest ion-diffusion path inside the MABM-GQD NPs.

Represented drug release study explains the releasing profile of MABM-GQD NPs and its delivery to the target cells of cytoplasm through diffusion mechanism. On planar construction of synthesized nanocomposites, GQDs have an enormous surface area to volume ratio, which leads to high drug concentration on its targeted delivery. We have thus bonded the In this manner, MABM aromatic ring structure with GQD by exclusive π-orbitals in the sp2-hybridized GQD frame through π–π stacking or electrostatic binding, which augments the chemically engineered GQD's application on targeted drug delivery. 28

Excitingly, MABM loaded on GQD showed an extremely good inhibitory effect for α-syn PFFs in vitro. The hydrophobic interactions of the graphene sheet and negatively charged carboxyl groups of GQD in contact with the positively charged region of α-syn induce destruction of fibril structure. 14 Our results indicates that MABM-GQD NPs significantly reduces the α-syn aggregation by interrupting the toxicity pathway of α-syn monomers that are assembled for fibrillar arrangement, arresting the intracellular protein inclusions storage. 29 The α-syn fibril length and its quantity displayed a concentration-dependent inhibition mode. 30 when incubated with MABM-GQD NPs. Electron microscopy studies revealed a front view of α-syn PFFs inhibition by its little broken hair-like structure, and SDS PAGE analysis and AFM confirms that α-syn PFFs incubated with MABM-GQD NPs should be converted to non-toxic form as the low molecular weight band disappears. 31

FTIR shows how the molecular conformations of the α-syn structure have been disturbed by NPs incubation. CD and Raman spectra of α-syn aggregates revealed that the vast majority of amide signals are identified with β-sheet arrangement and CH2-CH3 deformity confirms shut off the aggregation pathway. The amide-III peak shifting in α-syn relates to weak non-covalent association with MABM-GQD NPs, whereas amide-I signal results in distortion of amide linkages due to α-helices or disordered α-syn structures. 32 Electrochemical examination using the Nyquist plot form a semicircle in the high-frequency region and a slope in the low-frequency region, as its own electrochemical principle. The reduced existence of semi-circular arcs for MABM-GQD NPs depends upon the influence of α-syn fibril degradation. The occurrence may be due to positively charged amino acids in α-syn that interact with negatively charged acidic and hydroxyl groups of nanocomposite surfaces. 33 Therefore spectral results confirm the disordered α-syn fibril structure using engineered GQD; in turn, it leads to a functional inability to cause PD. Molecular dynamics simulation explores the negatively charged MABM-GQD NPs and makes strong interaction between N-terminal parts of α-syn fibrils while also decreasing the interaction between membrane and oligomeric α-syn. Furthermore, it alters the electrostatic interactions between α-syn fibrils, α-SOs, and anionic lipids, which lead to instability in compactness of structure, making it toxicity inefficient. 14

In vitro cytotoxicity of MABM-GQD NPs on neuroblastoma cell line are much less toxic. The controversial effect was identified using high reduction power on MTT assay and decreased cell damage dropdown the LDH release on NPs-treated with α-syn. 14 Evidence of cell viability can be explained by the way calcein enters viable cells and emits green fluorescence when it is cleaved by esterase, it can no longer permeate cell membranes. A level of superoxide anions generated in the neuronal cell was raised due to NPs oxidising the 2-hydroethidium into 2-hydroxyethidium, followed by generated superoxide anions that bind to DNA and become strong red fluorescent. For DAPI staining, nuclear morphology was more intact under MABM-GQD NPs treated with α-syn PFFs compared with others. 34 BBB crossing capacity of MABM-GQD NPs have been visualised as high incidence on the brain side. The reason for this result depicts is that positively charged nanocomposite may non-covalently interact on the negatively charged cell surface and internalize through an adsorptive endocytic path, which is called the endocytosis process. 35 This facilitates the low molecular weight nanocarriers that are used as targets for the drug delivery and its wide applications.

Preclinical studies have evaluate the above mentioned interactions between α-syn PFF and engineered GQD, and its contribution to the degradation of fibrillar region to a non-toxic soluble form. The ameliorative effect of the nanocomposite persuades the oxidative modification of α-syn or autophagy inhibition thereby and, may induce microglial pro-inflammatory responses. The level of reactive glial cells was significantly reduced by MABM-GQD NPs administration in α-syn PFFs-induced mice. The improvement in TH+ signal using nanocomposites prevents dopaminergic neurons from neurodegeneration. 36 CBF analysis through MRI scans provide evidence for recovering the effect of PD through α-syn inhibition. 37,38 Slowing the disease progression through MRI-guided neuroprotective drugs, MABM-GQD NPs offer hope for PD treatment. Commencement signs and symptoms of PD, SNC neurons that have degenerated cause anatomical changes.

Based on our results, we believe MABM-GQD nanocomposite could have a potential therapeutic effect on α-syn defibrillation and can also work to target specific drug delivery in brain. The evidence is supported by our animal studies, which used MRI envisages to enhance signal intensity and its neuroprotective effect, besides it might restore the future vision too principally stand against neurodegenerative disorders.
Poonkuzhali K.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation, Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation, Editing and Funding acquisition.
Seenivasagan R.: Docking, Simulation and Software.
Karthika A.: Methodology and editing.
Bukola Rhoda Aremu: Review and editing.
